summary: This was a public informational session titled 'Chat with the Chiefs' regarding the 2024 Goshen Fire and Ambulance replacement levy. Fire Chief Pegram and Chief Meyers presented the history of fire/EMS levies in Goshen Township, explained the current 4.5-mil levy expiring December 31, 2024, detailed rising operational costs due to inflation, and answered questions from residents about the proposed replacement levy and its impact on taxes and future services.
action_items: Chief Meyers to email Rich Starnow (and potentially other interested voters) year-over-year budget details, growth forecasting projections, and staffing plans for the next five years
Fire department leadership to provide year-over-year actual budget expenditure data since 2018 to resident Dave who requested it
key_amounts: $2.9 million total annual tax budget revenue for fire department
$600,000+ annual EMS billing revenue
$3.5 million approximate total annual fire department budget
$1,427,720.80 current levy revenue certified for 2024
$2,122,835 projected new levy revenue if approved
$695,114.20 increase in levy revenue (48.85% increase on this levy, 22.72% total budget increase)
$325,000/year in mortgage payments for Station 19, ambulances, and fire truck
$625,000 annual part-time employee costs
$400,000/year from fire levy dating back to volunteer era
$500,000/year from EMS levies (1974 and 1994)
$400,000/year fire department share of safety services levy (2002)
$1.6 million approximate current fire and EMS levy revenue
$250,000 ambulance cost in 2020, now $340,000
$625,000 fire engine cost in 2020, now $900,000
$340,000 estimated SCBA replacement cost
$70,000 per cardiac monitor (doubled in price)
$4,200 per set of fire gear (up from $2,000-$3,400)
$72,000 budgeted for fuel (up from $40,000-$42,000)
$2 million+ in grants received over past 10 years
$184,000 grant received with Loveland for physicals and health/wellness
$115.55 annual cost increase for average $204,300 home
$34,000 new construction revenue increase 2019-2024
$64,000+ saved by chief absorbing deputy chief role
$36,000/year insurance cost
notable_topics: History of failed fire/EMS levies in 2008, 2010, and 2012 and resulting service cuts
2024 replacement levy for expiring 4.5-mil fire and EMS levy
Inflationary cost increases for medications, equipment, vehicles, and fuel
Fixed-rate levy mechanics and how property value increases reduce millage
Staffing challenges with part-time personnel recruitment and retention
Affordable Care Act impact limiting part-time hours to 1,500/year
Consequences of levy failure including loss of 40 part-time employees and closing a fire station
Mutual aid billing implications for residents if levy fails
Comparison of call volume with neighboring departments (3,000+ runs with 2 stations)
Township growth projections and impact on fire/EMS services
General fund limitations and inability to sustain fire department operations
Competitive pay challenges versus neighboring departments like Miami Township and Kettering
New construction revenue lag and minimal budget impact
attendees: Bob (township official, referenced new construction windfall term)
notes: This was not a regular Board of Trustees meeting but a public informational forum ('Chat with the Chiefs') to educate voters about the fire and EMS replacement levy on the 2024 ballot. The session coincided with a school bond issue also on the ballot. Reference was made to Ray Schneider's Bible being present. The department won EMS Agency of the Year for Ohio in 2023. The department currently has a 36-year-old fire truck still in service. A medic unit was totaled in July of the previous year. The township was described as having only a couple hundred thousand dollars in general fund reserves compared to similarly-sized townships with $10-20 million unencumbered.
title: Fire and EMS Levy 2024 "Chat with the Chiefs"
